description:
# Automatic Issue Refresh

## Problem Statement

When using Beadsmith, the Issue List currently shows only a single snapshot of Beadwork's data — taken at the moment the app launches. If the underlying Beadwork state changes while Beadsmith is open (because the user ran `bw create` or `bw close` in another terminal, because a coding agent is creating or closing issues in the background, or because `bw sync` brought in changes from a remote), Beadsmith's view falls out of date silently. The user sees stale data with no indication that something changed and no way to ask for a refresh.

For a single-user desktop client where the user is doing real work, stale data is more than a minor inconvenience: it can mean the user acts on a list that doesn't reflect what they're actually working on. They might miss a new issue an agent just created, fail to see a comment that was added, or pick up a task that was just closed by another process.

## Solution

Make the Issue List stay in sync with Beadwork automatically. Beadsmith watches the local `refs/heads/beadwork` git ref — the canonical "data version marker" for Beadwork — and re-reads the issues whenever the ref moves. The user does not need to do anything; the list updates in place as the underlying state changes.

Two kinds of changes get caught:

- **Data changes** (someone ran `bw create`, `bw close`, `bw sync`, etc.) — caught by polling the ref every 2 seconds.
- **Time changes** (a deferred issue's `defer_until` boundary passes, moving it into Ready) — caught by a 60-second time-driven tick.

When the user comes back to a backgrounded Beadsmith window, a focus event triggers an immediate refresh. When the user switches workspaces, Beadsmith shows the new workspace's current state with a single, immediate load — no waiting on a poll tick, no stale data from the previous workspace.

Failures are absorbed silently up to a threshold (5 consecutive transient errors ≈ 10 seconds) and only surface as a non-disruptive banner above the list. Structural errors (missing `git` or `bw`, not a Beadwork workspace) surface immediately via the same banner, with a clear message. There is no manual refresh button. The system either works automatically or it tells the user why it cannot.

## User Stories

1. As a user with Beadsmith open, I want changes I make to Beadwork from another terminal to appear in Beadsmith within a few seconds, so that I am always looking at the current state of my work.
2. As a user, I want a coding agent creating or closing issues in the background to be reflected in Beadsmith automatically, so that I do not have to coordinate with the agent's view.
3. As a user running `bw sync` from the command line, I want Beadsmith to pick up the merged result without me reloading the app, so that I can keep working without interruption.
4. As a user coming back to a backgrounded Beadsmith window, I want to see the freshest state, so that I do not act on data that is stale from when I last looked.
5. As a user, I want a deferred issue whose `defer_until` boundary passes while Beadsmith is open to move into Ready without waiting for a new commit, so that the time-sensitive Ready view is always accurate.
6. As a user switching between workspaces, I want Beadsmith to show the new workspace's data immediately, so that I do not see the previous workspace's issues for a moment before the switch takes.
7. As a user, I want stale refresh events from a workspace I just left to be dropped, so that I never see the previous workspace's data flash across the new workspace's view.
8. As a user, I want a transient glitch (a one-time `git` lock, a slow `bw` invocation) to not show me an error, so that I am not interrupted by passing problems.
9. As a user with a persistent failure (multiple consecutive errors), I want a banner explaining the problem above the list, not the list replaced with a red error, so that I can still see my last known good state.
10. As a user, I want a missing `git` binary to surface as a clear banner, so that I understand why automatic refresh is not working and can install it.
11. As a user, I want a missing `bw` binary to surface as a clear banner, so that I understand the entire Beadwork integration is not working.
12. As a user, I want a non-Beadwork directory to surface as a clear error, so that I understand the workspace needs to be initialized.
13. As a user, I want the automatic refresh to be invisible when it works, so that the UI does not feel busy or jittery.
14. As a user, I want Beadsmith to do nothing (no polling, no errors) when no workspace is set, so that the empty state is genuinely empty.
15. As a user who switched away from a workspace and back, I want Beadsmith to reload only if the workspace's data actually changed, so that switching back is fast when nothing is new.
16. As a developer debugging the refresh logic, I want the loader, the scheduler, and the error classifier to be unit-testable in isolation, so that I can verify the timing and error behavior without a live `bw`.
17. As a maintainer, I want a clear, well-defined event contract between the backend and the frontend for refresh updates, so that the integration is debuggable and versionable.
18. As a maintainer, I want the data the loader sees to be scoped to the workspace the user is currently looking at, so that background work for a previously-active workspace cannot corrupt the visible state.
19. As a user, I want a refresh in progress to not be interrupted or duplicated when a focus event or a new data change arrives during the load, so that the system does not thrash on rapid back-to-back changes.
20. As a user, I want the data shown in the Issue List to never regress to an older state if events arrive out of order, so that I always see the most recent known data.

## Implementation Decisions

### Architecture

- A single refresh service in the Rust backend, bound to the Current Workspace. The service is idle when no workspace is set.
- The service has three trigger sources: data-change (2s `git rev-parse` poll), time-tick (60s timer), and window-focus-gain.
- All three triggers route through one single-flight loader that runs the existing Beadwork loaders (`bw list --all --json`, `bw ready --json`, `bw blocked --json`).
- The service emits refresh events; the frontend listens and replaces its state.
- For tests, the loader's external behavior is observable through a Tauri event stream; the scheduler is testable via fake clock; the subprocess calls are testable via the existing `CommandRunner` seam extended to handle `git`.

### Modules to be added

- A backend `RefreshService` that owns the scheduler, the per-workspace `last_seen_sha` map, and the generation counter.
- A backend `RefProbe` abstraction over `git rev-parse`, so tests can return SHAs without spawning a real `git`.
- A backend error-classifier that maps `CommandRunner` outcomes into the per-class failure table.
- A Tauri event channel carrying the refresh payload.
- A frontend subscription to the event channel that checks the generation token and replaces the `IssueExplorerLoadState`.

### Event contract

- **Event name:** `beadwork://issue-explorer-state-changed` (Tauri 2).
- **Success payload shape:** workspace path (string), generation (monotonically increasing integer per workspace), observed SHA (string), and the full `IssueExplorerData` (a structured object containing the all-issues list, the ready list, and the blocked list).
- **Failure payload shape:** workspace path (string), generation (integer), observed SHA (string or null), error kind (one of the existing `IssueListErrorKind` values), error message (string), and a transient flag (boolean).
- The frontend drops events whose `workspacePath` or `generation` does not match the active one. This is the workspace guard; it is the contract, not a heuristic.

### Error classification

| Class | Example | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| ref probe failure | `git rev-parse` exit non-zero, lock, IO | Transient, 5-strike rule, banner after |
| loader failure | any of `bw list/ready/blocked` exit non-zero | Transient, 5-strike rule, banner after |
| missing `git` | spawn `ENOENT` for `git` binary | Structural, banner immediately |
| missing `bw` | spawn `ENOENT` for `bw` binary | Structural, banner immediately |
| no active workspace | backend has no Current Workspace | Service is idle; no error, no banner |
| workspace deleted during operation | path becomes invalid | Treated as no active workspace; service becomes idle |

The 5-strike rule: 5 consecutive failures of the same transient class accumulate over approximately 10 seconds at 2s polling. While accumulating, the last good state is preserved silently. After 5, a banner above the list renders while the last good state remains visible.

### Workspace lifecycle

- The refresh service starts when a workspace becomes the Current Workspace.
- It runs for the lifetime of the workspace selection, with the per-workspace `last_seen_sha` map keying the data-change check.
- It stops (becomes idle) when the workspace is unset or switched away from.
- A new workspace gets a fresh generation; events for the old generation are dropped by the frontend.

### Loader behavior

- The loader runs the three `bw` calls in sequence (no parallelism needed; current cost is acceptable).
- If a reload is in flight when a new trigger arrives, a dirty flag is set; the loader re-runs once after the active load completes if the data is still stale.
- The loader emits its event only after all three calls have returned, even if one or more fail (partial-failure events carry the partial data plus the failure metadata).

### User-facing UX

- The user sees no spinner, toast, or other indicator on a successful automatic refresh. The list updates in place.
- The initial-load spinner stays for the first-ever load of a session.
- The workspace switch UX clears the previous workspace's data immediately and shows a loading state for the new workspace, then the new data.
- The 5-strike banner and the structural-error banner both render above the list, not as a replacement.

### Dependencies

- Beadsmith now depends on a `git` binary in addition to `bw`. ADR-0003 established `bw` as the structured-output integration surface; this design extends that surface to `git` for the ref-tip probe. The structured-output contract with `bw` is unchanged.
- If `git` is missing, the user sees a banner explaining Beadsmith needs `git` to detect changes automatically. This is the only place the new dependency surfaces in the UI.

## Testing Decisions

### What makes a good test for this feature

- Test **external behavior** — the observable outcome the user sees, not the internal implementation. A test that asserts "after a fake `bw create` and 2.5s of fake clock, the frontend received a refresh event with the new issue" is good. A test that asserts "the `RefreshService` called `tokio::time::sleep` exactly 2 times" is bad.
- Test the **risky cases**, not the happy path only. The race conditions and failure modes are where the bugs live.
- One assertion per test where possible. Tests that bundle multiple assertions are harder to read and refactor.
- Fakes at the highest seam: the `CommandRunner` is already a trait, the scheduler accepts a clock, and the Tauri event channel can be subscribed to in tests. Use these.

### Modules to be tested

- **RefProbe** (the `git rev-parse` wrapper): unit tests with a fake `CommandRunner`. Assert it returns the right SHA, returns the right error for missing ref vs. command failure, and handles worktree `.git` files.
- **RefreshService scheduler**: unit tests with a fake clock. Assert the 2s data poll, 60s time tick, and focus gain each fire as expected; assert the loader is single-flight with the dirty-bit re-run.
- **Error classifier**: unit tests for each failure class. Assert the right kind, the right transience flag, and the right counter behavior.
- **Event emitter**: integration test that an emitted event carries the workspace path, generation, and observed SHA, and that the frontend drops events with stale generations.
- **Frontend subscription**: unit test that the event handler replaces state atomically (event N+1 arriving before N does not regress state).
- **Workspace switch**: integration test that an in-flight loader from workspace A does not overwrite workspace B's state.

### Prior art for the tests

- The existing `CommandRunner` trait tests in the backend adapter show the pattern: `FakeRunner` records invocations and returns canned `CommandOutput`. The new ref-probe tests follow the same shape.
- The existing `IssueTransport` test pattern in the frontend loader shows how the frontend mocks the RPC layer. The new event-subscription tests follow the same shape, with the addition of awaiting events from the Tauri channel.
- The wdio Issue List slice (per the project's webdriver e2e docs) provides a prior-art integration test that exercises the live UI. The new "auto-refresh works without user action" e2e test follows the same pattern, with a wait-for-state helper that does not rely on fixed sleeps.

## Out of Scope

- Collapsing the three `bw` loaders into a single `bw state --json` call. Acceptable cost; separate ADR if pursued.
- Configurable polling interval. Hard-coded at 2s for now.
- Pause-when-minimized behavior. Focus-gain already covers the "user comes back" case.
- A `bw watch` command in Beadwork. Deferred to upstream.
- Detecting changes from a remote that has not been synced locally. The poller only sees the local ref.
- A user-facing "last refreshed" timestamp. The user sees no indicator on success.
- Persisting the per-workspace `last_seen_sha` map across app restarts. It lives in memory; on restart, the next tick will detect any drift.
- Per-issue deltas. The frontend receives the full refreshed state each time; the implementation does not compute what changed.

## Further Notes

- The full design is in ADR-0007.
- The glossary entry for `Ready` was updated to record that Ready is time-sensitive (data + time, not data only). The 60s time tick exists to handle the time-only changes.
- This epic is blocked by `bsm-xjm` (the workspace management epic) in the issue tracker.
- The lazygit precedent (jesseduffield/lazygit PR #5662, commits `7c22c52` and `3050303`) validates the 2-second polling approach for a UI client watching local git state.
title: Automatically refresh issues from Beadwork
